summary: This informal blog post is primarily a collection of announcements, disclaimers, and brief observations. Joel promotes upcoming meetups and lectures, addresses reader reactions to previous posts, and shares advice on resume writing. The core substantive content argues that job applicants should present themselves as human beings rather than lists of technical skills. Joel suggests that telling a personal story in a cover letter creates an emotional connection that makes it harder for hiring managers to dismiss candidates. The post reflects Joel's characteristic blend of community building, humor, and practical career advice.
key_points: The best way to get a hiring manager's attention is to come across as a human being rather than a list of technologies and job titles.
Personal stories in cover letters create emotional connections that change the dynamic between applicant and reviewer.
Joel addresses criticism from a Slashdot audience, noting that people without a sense of humor invariably believe they have a great one.
The Joel on Software community was actively organizing real-world meetups across 28 cities worldwide.
money_quotes: The number one best way to get someone to look at your resume closely: come across as a human being, not a list of jobs and programming languages.
I just can't care about "C/C++/Perl/ASP" in the same way.
So, maybe you won't be qualified for the job, but it's just a lot harder for me to dismiss you out of hand.
I can't read them without thinking of these people as human beings. And now the dynamic has changed. I like you. I care about you.
The one thing common among all people without a sense of humor is that they inevitably think they have a very good sense of humor; your joke just wasn't funny.
key_insight: Resumes and cover letters that tell a human story create an emotional connection that a list of technical skills never can, making it harder for hiring managers to dismiss candidates out of hand.
